summary: First auditor pass on runtime2-generator-obp covering coder/v5 (security #1 [Sensitive] masking + #3 cycle/depth → ServiceError). codeanalyzer/v3 cleared coder/v3, tester/v4 approved coder/v4, security/v1 passed coder/v4 — none reviewed v5. v5 fixes are honest closures of security findings #1 and #3, with one cross-file contract gap: the new Data.As<T> ServiceError contract is honored by legacy property emission (sets __resolutionError) but not by Data<T> property emission (assigns FromError to backing field, error invisible to Run()). Practical impact small — cycles in user variables are rare — but the contract is now inconsistent between the two emission paths and the test comments claim error visibility that only legacy provides. Verdict: PASS. C# 2468/2468 green.

previous_reviews_assessed.codeanalyzer: agree — production code on coder/v3 deltas (cycle, depth, OCE asymmetry, diagnostic span) is clean; Finding 46 NIT honestly closed in v4.
previous_reviews_assessed.tester: agree — Pattern B regex+helper rewrite with 9 deletion-tested contracts is honest closure; strip-integration observation precedent-aligned with Pattern A's gap, appropriate to leave as observation.
previous_reviews_assessed.security: partial — finding #3 fix is half-completed: legacy emission surfaces As<T> ServiceErrors via __resolutionError; Data<T> emission buries them on the backing field. See auditor Finding 1.

findings.issue: v5's contract change in Data.AsT_Impl (cycle/depth-trip → FromError(ServiceError)) is honored by the legacy property emission path (__Resolve<T> sets __resolutionError, ExecuteAsync short-circuits before Run()) but NOT by the Data<T> property emission path. The Data<T> getter assigns __ResolveData(...).As<T>(Context) directly to the backing field; if As<T> returned FromError, the FromError-Data lives on the backing field with Error set, but the handler's Run() body reads .Value (default(T) — null) and proceeds without seeing the error. Test comments on the renamed cycle tests claim 'callers see the failure rather than an unresolved %var% leak' — true only for legacy properties. New handlers all use Data<T>.
findings.impact: Cycles or expanding chains > 32 deep in user variables silently produce null-Value handler runs instead of surfacing the cycle/depth error. Practical impact low (rare scenarios), but the contract inconsistency between the two emission paths is a footgun for the first handler that depends on loud failure for cyclic %var% references. The Data<T> path is the forward-looking emission (Phase 5 will delete legacy).
findings.suggestion: Mirror the legacy pattern in each Data<T> emission branch: after the As<T> call, branch on Success and route to __resolutionError. Single-line addition per branch (lines 32, 36, 40, 44 of PLang.Generators/Emission/Property/Data/this.cs). Add a Data<T> handler test that pins the contract: cyclic %var% resolution → handler returns ServiceError to the caller (not just an Error stamped on the backing Data).

findings.issue: SnapshotOnError_SensitiveProperty_UnaccessedStillMasksPrValue is misnamed — the SensitiveSnapshot handler always touches both ApiKey.Value and Endpoint.Value in Run(), so __X_set is always true. The test pins the PrValue null-guard branch (when __pr.Value is null), but the unaccessed-AND-sensitive-AND-FinalValue branch (where {SetFlag} guard is the only thing keeping FinalValue from being '******' for an untouched property) has zero deletion-test coverage. Coder's own comment acknowledges this trade-off.
findings.impact: If a future change drops the {SetFlag} guard from the masked FinalValue expression and emits (object?)'******' unconditionally, no test would fail. Low blast radius — the non-sensitive variant of the same guard is pinned by SnapshotOnError_UnaccessedProperty_FinalValueNull, so a regression would leave the non-sensitive contract intact and only affect over-masking precision.
findings.suggestion: Add a second [Sensitive] matrix handler that takes a parameter without touching it in Run() (returns FromError immediately), then assert apiKey.WasAccessed IsFalse && apiKey.FinalValue IsNull. Or rename the existing test to SnapshotOnError_SensitiveProperty_NullPrValue_StaysNull to honestly describe what it pins.

findings.issue: Sensitive FinalValue expression is `{SetFlag} ? (object?)"******" : null` — masks regardless of whether the resolved backing value is null. Non-sensitive form is `{SetFlag} ? (object?){Backing} : null` which preserves null. So a sensitive property that was accessed but resolved to null reports FinalValue='******' as if there were a secret to redact. Same shape in Legacy emission (line 82).
findings.impact: Slight over-masking, no security concern (never under-masks). Misleading for post-mortem analysis — distinguishing 'accessed-and-null' from 'accessed-and-redacted' is informative.
findings.suggestion: Optionally tighten to `{SetFlag} ? ({Backing} != null ? (object?)"******" : null) : null` to mirror the PrValue null-guard pattern. NIT — precision improvement, not blocking.

findings.issue: SensitiveAttribute matched by short name only: `a.AttributeClass?.Name == "SensitiveAttribute"`. Same convention as the existing ProviderAttribute / VariableNameAttribute matches in the same method. A developer declaring a different SensitiveAttribute in another namespace (e.g., MyApp.Custom.SensitiveAttribute) would inadvertently trigger masking. The runtime SensitivePropertyFilter matches by full type via IsDefined(typeof(SensitiveAttribute), false) — the two layers disagree on attribute identity.
findings.impact: Theoretical only. No current attribute name collision in the codebase.
findings.suggestion: Existing convention in Discovery — fixing this for SensitiveAttribute alone would create a different inconsistency. Worth knowing for any future standardization on fully-qualified attribute matching in source generators.
